content.text: What It Is

A Service Level Agreement (SLA) is a contractual commitment between an AI platform provider and its customers that defines the minimum acceptable performance standards for reliability, responsiveness, and support. SLA definition is the process of specifying what is promised; SLA tracking is the continuous measurement of whether those promises are being kept. Together they form the operational backbone of a production AI service contract.

For AI products, SLAs span three distinct dimensions: uptime (the fraction of time the service is available and returning valid responses), response time (how quickly a support team acknowledges a new ticket), and resolution time (how quickly an issue is fixed). Each dimension requires different measurement instrumentation, different alerting thresholds, and different remediation playbooks when targets are missed.

SLA tracking differs from general system monitoring. System monitoring tells engineers when something is wrong. SLA tracking tells the business whether contractual obligations are being met, which customers are at risk of breach-credit obligations, and what trends predict future breaches before they happen. The distinction matters because the audience and the action differ: a latency spike triggers an ops alert; the same spike integrated over time may trigger a customer credit and a board-level incident report.
Why It Matters

In competitive enterprise AI markets, SLA commitments are often a purchasing requirement. Enterprise buyers will not deploy AI systems in production pipelines without written guarantees about uptime and support responsiveness. Without an SLA, the vendor cannot compete for enterprise contracts. With a poorly defined or unmeasured SLA, the vendor faces either reputational damage when performance falls short of informal expectations, or credit obligations it cannot afford.

For the engineering team, formal SLA tracking creates accountability that informal monitoring does not. When uptime is measured and reported to customers monthly, degradation that would otherwise be absorbed as 'noise' becomes visible. Teams that track SLA compliance consistently invest more in reliability engineering — redundancy, graceful degradation, circuit breakers — because the cost of a missed SLA is now concrete and visible.

For customers, SLA tracking provides the evidence base for internal reporting to their own stakeholders. An enterprise AI deployment justifies its budget in part by demonstrating measured reliability. Monthly SLA reports from the vendor become inputs to the customer's own internal performance dashboards and renewal justification cycles.
Benefits
Contractual clarity: A written SLA eliminates ambiguity about what 'reliable' means, reducing disputes about whether the vendor has met its obligations.
Trust building: Monthly SLA reports sent proactively demonstrate transparency and build the long-term trust that underpins contract renewals.
Internal investment signal: Measured breach rates create internal pressure on engineering teams to prioritize reliability work over feature development when reliability is lagging.
Competitive differentiation: A verifiable 99.9% uptime SLA with automated credit calculation is a sales advantage over vendors whose SLAs are informal or unmeasured.
Early warning system: SLA tracking at the ticket level — measuring elapsed fraction rather than waiting for breach — gives lead time to escalate before the commitment is missed.
Credit obligation predictability: Formal measurement of breaches and automated credit calculation eliminates manual disputes and makes credit costs predictable for finance.
Trade-offs

Legal and financial exposure: A formal SLA creates enforceable obligations. If the service degrades, the vendor owes credits. A poorly set SLA target that engineering cannot reliably meet is a recurring financial liability.

Measurement complexity: Uptime must be measured from the customer's perspective, not from internal infrastructure monitoring. Internal metrics that exclude load balancer faults or DNS issues overstate uptime.

Exclusion disputes: Scheduled maintenance, force majeure, and third-party dependency outages are typically excluded from SLA calculations, but customers often dispute these exclusions during incidents.

Tier management overhead: Supporting multiple SLA tiers (enterprise, managed, self-serve) requires routing logic that assigns tickets to different queues and applies different SLA deadlines, adding operational complexity.
Credit accounting: Automated credit systems must integrate with billing infrastructure, which is non-trivial in many enterprise billing stacks.
How It Works
Uptime SLA

The uptime SLA is defined as the percentage of time within a measurement window (typically a rolling 30-day calendar month) during which the service is available and responding correctly. Downtime is defined as any period during which the service returns error responses or is unreachable from the customer's perspective, excluding scheduled maintenance windows and force-majeure events.
The formula for uptime SLA percentage is:

Where t_up is the total up-time in the measurement window and t_down is the total down-time, both measured in the same unit (minutes is standard). For a 30-day month with 43,200 total minutes, an SLA target of 99.9% means the service may be down for at most 43.2 minutes before the SLA is breached.
Converting SLA percentages to allowable downtime is a practical tool for setting realistic targets:
99.9% (three nines): ~43 minutes downtime per month, ~8.7 hours per year
99.5% (two-and-a-half nines): ~216 minutes per month, ~43.8 hours per year
99.0% (two nines): ~432 minutes per month, ~87.6 hours per year
99.95% (three-and-a-half nines): ~21.6 minutes per month, ~4.4 hours per year
Response Time SLA

The response time SLA measures how quickly the support team sends the first substantive acknowledgment — a message from a human engineer, not an automated receipt — after a ticket is opened. Response time SLA is measured per ticket, reported as the fraction of tickets that met the target within the window. A response time SLA of 99% for P0 tickets means that 99% of P0 tickets received a first response within the agreed time (e.g., 15 minutes for enterprise tier).
Resolution Time SLA

The resolution time SLA measures how long it takes from ticket creation to the customer confirming the issue is resolved. Unlike response time, resolution time depends on investigation complexity and fix deployment time, making it harder to guarantee. Resolution SLAs are therefore typically set conservatively relative to actual median resolution times. The SLA target should be at or above the 90th percentile of historical resolution times to remain achievable under normal conditions while still being a meaningful commitment.
SLA Tiers

Different customer segments receive different SLA commitments based on contract value, use case criticality, and support capacity. Tier definitions are published in the product's SLA addendum and referenced in the master subscription agreement.
Architecture
The diagram below shows the three-tier SLA structure and the tracking pipeline from ticket creation through resolution and monthly reporting.

attrs.alt: SLA tiered tracking dashboard showing three tiers (Enterprise, Managed, Self-serve) with severity rows and the SLA tracking pipeline below

content.text: When to Use It

Formal SLA definition and tracking is appropriate as soon as the product has at least one paying customer who depends on the service for business-critical operations. For pre-revenue products or internal tooling, informal availability targets are sufficient. Once the first enterprise contract is signed, the SLA addendum must be in place before go-live.

Self-serve SLAs are appropriate for products with a freemium model: the commitment is lower, the measurement is the same, and it establishes a baseline that can be upgraded when customers move to paid tiers. Do not offer identical SLA commitments across tiers — differentiated SLA tiers are a pricing lever and a capacity planning tool.

Avoid committing to an uptime SLA that exceeds the reliability of your critical dependencies. If the underlying model API has a documented 99.5% uptime, an application-level 99.9% SLA is not achievable without a fallback model or a caching layer that can serve requests during model API downtime.
How to Implement
Step 1: Define the SLA tiers in configuration
Codify the SLA commitments in a version-controlled YAML configuration file. This file becomes the source of truth for both the legal addendum and the tracking system.

# sla-config.yaml
tiers:
  enterprise:
    uptime_sla_pct: 99.9
    support_channel: [dedicated_slack, email, phone]
    csm: assigned
    severities:
      P0:
        first_response_minutes: 15
        resolution_hours: 4
        update_interval_minutes: 30
      P1:
        first_response_hours: 1
        resolution_hours: 8
        update_interval_hours: 2
      P2:
        first_response_hours: 4
        resolution_days: 2
      P3:
        first_response_days: 1
        resolution_days: 5

  managed:
    uptime_sla_pct: 99.5
    support_channel: [email, portal]
    csm: shared
    severities:
      P0:
        first_response_minutes: 30
        resolution_hours: 8
      P1:
        first_response_hours: 4
        resolution_days: 2
      P2:
        first_response_days: 1
        resolution_days: 5
      P3:
        first_response_days: 2
        resolution_days: 10

  self_serve:
    uptime_sla_pct: 99.0
    support_channel: [portal, docs, community]
    csm: none
    severities:
      P0:
        first_response_hours: 2
        resolution_days: 1
      P1:
        first_response_days: 1
        resolution_days: 5
      P2:
        first_response_days: 3
        resolution_days: 10
      P3:
        first_response_days: 5
        resolution_days: 30

measurement:
  uptime_window: monthly          # rolling 30-day window
  exclusions:
    - scheduled_maintenance
    - force_majeure
  credits:
    breach_threshold_pct: 0.1     # > 0.1% downtime triggers credits
    credit_table:
      - range: [99.0, 99.9]
        credit_pct: 10
      - range: [95.0, 99.0]
        credit_pct: 25
      - range: [0, 95.0]
        credit_pct: 50
Step 2: Instrument uptime measurement

Deploy a synthetic monitoring probe that makes a real API call (a simple, inexpensive call like a token-count request) to the production endpoint every 60 seconds from multiple geographic regions. Record the result (success or failure) and timestamp. Compute uptime from the probe history rather than from internal infrastructure metrics to match the customer's experience.
Step 3: Implement ticket-level SLA tracking

Wire SLA deadlines to ticket creation timestamps in the ticketing system. Use the tier and severity fields to look up the SLA commitment and compute the deadline. Build a background job that checks elapsed fraction every minute and fires alerts at 50%, 75%, and 90% thresholds.
Step 4: Build the tracking dashboard
Aggregate ticket-level data into a dashboard that shows breach rate by tier and severity, rolling uptime SLA, and trend lines. Export monthly reports automatically.
Examples
Ticket-level SLA tracker (Python)

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class Tier(str, Enum):
    ENTERPRISE = "enterprise"
    MANAGED = "managed"
    SELF_SERVE = "self_serve"

class Severity(str, Enum):
    P0 = "P0"
    P1 = "P1"
    P2 = "P2"
    P3 = "P3"

# Resolution SLA in hours keyed by (tier, severity)
RESOLUTION_SLA: dict[tuple[Tier, Severity], float] = {
    (Tier.ENTERPRISE, Severity.P0):   4,
    (Tier.ENTERPRISE, Severity.P1):   8,
    (Tier.ENTERPRISE, Severity.P2):   48,
    (Tier.ENTERPRISE, Severity.P3):   120,
    (Tier.MANAGED,    Severity.P0):   8,
    (Tier.MANAGED,    Severity.P1):   48,
    (Tier.MANAGED,    Severity.P2):   120,
    (Tier.MANAGED,    Severity.P3):   240,
    (Tier.SELF_SERVE, Severity.P0):   24,
    (Tier.SELF_SERVE, Severity.P1):   120,
    (Tier.SELF_SERVE, Severity.P2):   240,
    (Tier.SELF_SERVE, Severity.P3):   720,
}

@dataclass
class Ticket:
    ticket_id: str
    customer_tier: Tier
    severity: Severity
    created_at: datetime
    resolved_at: Optional[datetime] = None
    first_response_at: Optional[datetime] = None

    @property
    def sla_deadline(self) -> datetime:
        hours = RESOLUTION_SLA[(self.customer_tier, self.severity)]
        return self.created_at + timedelta(hours=hours)

    @property
    def elapsed_fraction(self) -> float:
        """Fraction of SLA window consumed (0.0 to >1.0)."""
        now = datetime.utcnow()
        total = (self.sla_deadline - self.created_at).total_seconds()
        elapsed = (now - self.created_at).total_seconds()
        return elapsed / total if total > 0 else 0.0

    @property
    def is_breached(self) -> bool:
        if self.resolved_at:
            return self.resolved_at > self.sla_deadline
        return datetime.utcnow() > self.sla_deadline


def compute_uptime_sla(
    t_up_minutes: float,
    t_down_minutes: float,
) -> float:
    """Return uptime SLA percentage."""
    total = t_up_minutes + t_down_minutes
    if total == 0:
        return 100.0
    return (t_up_minutes / total) * 100.0


# --- Example ---
ticket = Ticket(
    ticket_id="TKT-9001",
    customer_tier=Tier.ENTERPRISE,
    severity=Severity.P1,
    created_at=datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=6),
)
print(f"Ticket {ticket.ticket_id}")
print(f"  SLA deadline : {ticket.sla_deadline.isoformat()}")
print(f"  Elapsed frac : {ticket.elapsed_fraction:.1%}")
print(f"  Breached     : {ticket.is_breached}")

# Uptime SLA calculation
# 30-day month = 43200 minutes; 15 minutes of downtime
sla_pct = compute_uptime_sla(t_up_minutes=43185, t_down_minutes=15)
print(f"  Uptime SLA   : {sla_pct:.4f}%")  # -> 99.9653%
SLA dashboard aggregator (Python)

from collections import defaultdict
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import NamedTuple

class SLASummary(NamedTuple):
    tier: str
    severity: str
    total_tickets: int
    breached: int
    breach_rate_pct: float
    avg_resolution_hours: float
    p95_resolution_hours: float

def build_sla_dashboard(
    tickets: list[dict],
    window_start: datetime,
    window_end: datetime,
) -> list[SLASummary]:
    """
    Aggregate SLA performance metrics over a time window.
    Each ticket dict has: tier, severity, created_at, resolved_at, sla_deadline.
    """
    import statistics

    groups: dict[tuple, list[dict]] = defaultdict(list)
    for t in tickets:
        created = t["created_at"]
        if window_start <= created <= window_end:
            groups[(t["tier"], t["severity"])].append(t)

    summaries = []
    for (tier, sev), group in sorted(groups.items()):
        breached = [t for t in group if t["resolved_at"] > t["sla_deadline"]]
        resolved = [
            (t["resolved_at"] - t["created_at"]).total_seconds() / 3600
            for t in group if t.get("resolved_at")
        ]
        avg_res = statistics.mean(resolved) if resolved else 0.0
        p95_res = (
            sorted(resolved)[int(len(resolved) * 0.95)]
            if len(resolved) >= 20 else max(resolved, default=0.0)
        )
        summaries.append(SLASummary(
            tier=tier,
            severity=sev,
            total_tickets=len(group),
            breached=len(breached),
            breach_rate_pct=len(breached) / len(group) * 100 if group else 0.0,
            avg_resolution_hours=round(avg_res, 2),
            p95_resolution_hours=round(p95_res, 2),
        ))
    return summaries
Real-World Example

An enterprise AI platform offering document processing to legal firms defines three tiers. The Enterprise tier (100+ seat contracts) carries a 99.9% uptime SLA with a 15-minute P0 response time and a 4-hour resolution time. The Managed tier (10–99 seat contracts) carries 99.5% uptime with a 30-minute P0 response time. The Self-Serve tier (per-API-key billing) carries 99.0% uptime with 2-hour P0 response time and no guaranteed resolution time.

The uptime measurement is implemented as a synthetic probe running every 60 seconds from three AWS regions (us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1). A minute is counted as 'down' if at least two of three probes fail. This multi-region consensus prevents false positives from regional network issues that do not affect the customer. The probe results feed a time-series database; a daily job computes the rolling 30-day uptime percentage per tier and writes it to the SLA dashboard.

For ticket SLA tracking, the ticketing system (Linear) has a custom field for SLA deadline computed at ticket creation. A GitHub Actions cron job running every 5 minutes queries open tickets, computes elapsed fraction, and posts a Slack alert to the on-call channel at 75% and 90% elapsed. The monthly SLA report is generated by the Python aggregator and emailed to the assigned customer success manager for review before being sent to the customer, ensuring any unusual months receive a human-written explanation alongside the data.
Common Mistakes

Measuring uptime from internal infrastructure rather than the customer perspective: Internal monitoring misses load balancer faults, DNS failures, and TLS certificate expiry, which are visible to the customer but not to internal tools.

Setting uptime SLA targets above dependency reliability: An application-level 99.9% SLA backed by a third-party model API with 99.5% reliability requires a fallback strategy or the application will breach its SLA whenever the dependency does.

Not defining 'downtime' precisely in the contract: Vague definitions lead to disputes. The contract must specify exactly what constitutes a service failure (e.g., error rate above 5% for more than 60 consecutive seconds).

Conflating system metrics with SLA metrics: Internal P99 latency dashboards serve engineering; SLA compliance dashboards serve legal and commercial. They should be separate and the SLA dashboard should be customer-auditable.

Failing to account for exclusions in the tracking system: Scheduled maintenance must be automatically excluded from downtime calculations, or every maintenance window artificially depresses the measured SLA.

Not publishing the SLA measurement methodology: Customers who cannot verify the uptime calculation will dispute the numbers. Publish the probe frequency, the failure definition, and the aggregation window.
Best Practices
Use consensus probing from multiple regions to compute uptime: A single probe is vulnerable to false positives; two-of-three consensus significantly reduces false breach events.
Store raw probe data indefinitely: SLA disputes can arise months after an incident; raw probe data is the evidence required to reconstruct the exact downtime minutes.

Automate credit calculation: Manual credit calculations introduce errors and delay. Automate the credit amount based on the SLA percentage and the customer's monthly invoice value, subject to human review before issuance.
Set SLA targets below your actual capability: If the system reliably achieves 99.95% uptime, commit to 99.9%. The buffer absorbs occasional outlier months without triggering credits.
Separate SLA reporting from internal monitoring: The SLA dashboard is a commercial document; mixing it with operational dashboards creates confusion about audience and intent.
Review SLA commitments annually: As the system architecture evolves, the achievable uptime target changes. Annual review prevents stale commitments from becoming permanent liabilities.
Performance and Scalability

SLA tracking at scale requires careful attention to data volume and query performance. A platform with 10,000 active tickets and synthetic probing at 60-second intervals generates roughly 525,600 probe records per year per probe location. At three probe locations, that is 1.5 million records per year. A time-series database (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, or a cloud equivalent such as Amazon Timestream) is purpose-built for this workload and handles uptime aggregation queries in milliseconds at this scale.

For ticket-level SLA tracking, the background job that checks elapsed fractions must complete its scan of open tickets in under one minute to maintain alerting accuracy. At 10,000 open tickets, a simple indexed database query with a filter on is_open and created_at completes in well under a second. At 100,000 open tickets, partitioning by severity and processing each partition in parallel maintains the same latency budget.

Monthly SLA report generation can be batch-processed because it is not time-sensitive. Schedule the job at the first minute of each month for the prior month's data. Pre-aggregate the data into a summary table during the month rather than computing it all at month-end to reduce report generation time from minutes to milliseconds.
Tooling and Ecosystem

The SLA tracking stack typically spans several tool categories. Synthetic monitoring tools include Datadog Synthetics, Checkly, and AWS CloudWatch Synthetics, which all support multi-region probes with alerting and historical storage. For time-series uptime data, InfluxDB and TimescaleDB offer specialized aggregation functions. Ticketing integrations are available for Zendesk (via its SLA Policy feature), Linear (via custom fields and webhooks), and Jira Service Management (via built-in SLA tracking). PagerDuty handles the on-call routing for SLA breach alerts. SLA report generation can be implemented with the Python stack shown above or with business intelligence tools such as Metabase or Grafana that connect directly to the ticketing database.
Comparison with Alternatives
The table below compares formal SLA tracking with informal availability targets and with third-party SLA verification services.
Dimension
Formal SLA Tracking
Informal Targets
Third-Party Verification
Contractual force
Legally binding with credit obligations
None; no remedy for customer
Legally binding; audited by third party
Measurement authority
Vendor-operated probes; methodology published
None
Independent probes operated by auditor
Customer trust
Medium — vendor marks their own work
Low
High — independent evidence
Implementation cost
Low to medium — internal tooling
Near zero
High — auditor fees, integration work
Dispute resolution
Vendor provides raw data for audit
Disputes unresolved
Auditor data is authoritative
Typical enterprise requirement
Standard for most enterprise contracts
Insufficient
Required for regulated industries (finance, healthcare)
Security and Reliability

The SLA tracking system itself must be highly available. If the uptime probe infrastructure goes down, the measurement record has gaps, which creates disputes about whether the production service was actually up during that interval. Deploy the probing infrastructure in a separate account and region from the production system, with its own redundancy. Probe infrastructure downtime should be logged separately and is not included in the production SLA calculation.

SLA dashboards expose financial data (credit obligations) and contractual data (tier assignments). Access should be role-gated: support engineers see ticket-level SLA status; sales and finance see customer-level SLA compliance summaries; customers see only their own data via the customer portal. Do not expose aggregate SLA data across customers to any individual customer.

Immutable audit logs of all SLA calculations — probe results, downtime intervals, exclusion applications, and credit computations — are required for contract compliance and dispute resolution. Store these logs in write-once storage (S3 Object Lock, Azure Immutable Blob Storage) with a retention period matching the contract's statute of limitations.
Mental Model

Think of an SLA as a speedometer and a fuel gauge in a car. The speedometer (response time and resolution time SLA) tells the driver in real time whether they are meeting their obligations. The fuel gauge (uptime SLA percentage) shows how much margin remains before a contractual limit is hit. A driver who only looks at the speedometer may not notice they are running low on fuel until it is too late. An SLA tracking system that measures both dimensions gives the support and engineering teams the same dual visibility.

The tiered SLA structure maps to different road types: the enterprise lane is a private highway with strict speed limits and guaranteed service; the managed lane is a toll road with good but not guaranteed service; the self-serve lane is a public road where the driver is mostly on their own. Each lane has different rules, different costs, and different expectations. Putting a self-serve customer on the enterprise lane wastes capacity; putting an enterprise customer on the self-serve lane breaches the contract.
Key Takeaways
SLA tracking spans three dimensions — uptime, response time, and resolution time — each requiring different measurement instrumentation and different alerting strategies.
The uptime SLA formula is SLA = (t_up / (t_up + t_down)) * 100, where both times are measured from the customer's perspective using synthetic probes, not from internal infrastructure metrics.

SLA tiers (enterprise, managed, self-serve) encode different commitments and should be codified in version-controlled YAML that serves as the source of truth for both the legal addendum and the tracking system.
Setting uptime SLA targets above the reliability of critical dependencies creates commitments that cannot be met without a fallback strategy.
SLA breach alerting must fire at 75% elapsed, not at 100% — escalation requires lead time, and a breach that is noticed only after it happens cannot be prevented.
The SLA tracking system must itself be highly available and deployed independently from the production system it monitors, or probe downtime creates unresolvable disputes.
Monthly SLA reports sent proactively to customers demonstrate transparency and build the long-term commercial trust that underpins contract renewals.
